coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence turn-on assay for detection of serum D-penicillamine based on papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yifan
Qiao, Juan
Liu, Qianrong
Zhang, Mingming
Qi, Li
coredata.dc:description: Papain-stabilized gold nanoclusters (papain@AuNCs) with a red fluorescence emission at 639 nm have been generated successfully in aqueous solution. The fluorescence of papain@AuNCs could be quenched in the addition of Cu2+. Subsequently, a unique fluorescent probe based on papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex has been constructed for sensitive, selective and “turn-on” detection of D-penicillamine (D-pen). The sensing probe has exhibited a remarkable fluorescence enhancement in the presence of D-pen, which can be measured ranging from 30.0 μM to 2.0 mM (r2 = 0.991) with the detection limit of 5.0 μM. Furthermore, the developed assay has been utilized to explore the metabolic process of D-pen in rats after intraperitoneal injection. As far as we concerned, the present study has reported the first analytical method for sensing serum D-pen in real time by using the fluorescent papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex. Our strategy paves a way for digging up the biological and clinical application of the fluorescent AuNCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Papain@AuNCs
Fluorescence turn on
D-penicillamine
Metabolic process

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex for sensing D-pen
Principle of the turn-on fluorescence for sensing D-pen
Determination of D-pen in rat serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of papain@AuNCs; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of aqueous HAuCl4, papain and papain@AuNCs. Inset: photos of the synthesized papain@AuNCs under the visible light (c) and UV light (d).

Selectivity of the sensing system for D-pen detection against common metal ions, glucose, lactic acid (LA), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA) and amino acids (n = 3): 1.0 mM of Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Hg2+, ascorbic acid, uric acid; 2.0 mM of D-pen, lactic acid; 3.0 mM of Ca2+; 5.0 mM of K+, glucose; 100.0 mM of Na+; 20.0 μM of Glu, Cys, Met, Tyr, GSH; D-Tyr; 60.0 μM of Phe, Arg, Trp, Ser; 100.0 μM of H2O2, HSO3 −, S2−, ClO−, HOONO; 120.0 μM of Thr; 200.0 μM of Val, at pH 5.5.
Schematic illustration of the turn-on fluorescence for sensing D-pen based on papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex.

DLS of the as-prepared papain@AuNCs (A), papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex (B) and papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ in the presence of D-pen (C). TEM images of the as-prepared papain@AuNCs (D), papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex (E) and papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ in the presence of D-pen (F).
(A) Fluorescence spectra of papain@AuNCs in the presence of varied concentrations of D-pen; (B) Plot of the fluorescence intensity I - I0 versus the concentrations of D-pen (n = 3).

The metabolic assay of D-pen at different times in rat serum (n = 3) after injected into the abdominal cavity. Experimental conditions: three male SD rats (about 250 g) were selected, the controlled samples and five different serum samples (at 15 min, 30 min, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h) were collected after 400.0 mg/kg D-pen sulfate dissolved in physiological saline solution was injected into the abdominal cavity of rats.
The recovery of sensing D-pen in serum samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence turn-on assay for detection of serum D-penicillamine based on papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing National Laboratory of Molecular Sciences
Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Papain-stabilized gold nanoclusters (papain@AuNCs) with a red fluorescence emission at 639 nm have been generated successfully in aqueous solution. The fluorescence of papain@AuNCs could be quenched in the addition of Cu2+. Subsequently, a unique fluorescent probe based on papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex has been constructed for sensitive, selective and “turn-on” detection of D-penicillamine (D-pen). The sensing probe has exhibited a remarkable fluorescence enhancement in the presence of D-pen, which can be measured ranging from 30.0 μM to 2.0 mM (r2 = 0.991) with the detection limit of 5.0 μM. Furthermore, the developed assay has been utilized to explore the metabolic process of D-pen in rats after intraperitoneal injection. As far as we concerned, the present study has reported the first analytical method for sensing serum D-pen in real time by using the fluorescent papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex. Our strategy paves a way for digging up the biological and clinical application of the fluorescent AuNCs.

ce:section.ce:para: D-penicillamine (D-pen) is actually the drug most extensively used in heavy metal detoxification treatment, which could chelate the heavy metal ions (such as copper ions) and form complexes [1]. It also could be applied to treatment for diseases, such as Wilson's disease, primary biliary cirrhosis and progressive systemic sclerosis [2]. However, excessive D-pen in the blood could cause some side effects, such as myasthenia and thrombocytopenia [3]. Therefore, monitoring the concentration of D-pen in blood is important and necessary in disease therapies and in metabolic processes study.

Up to now, various analytical techniques have been utilized for the determination of D-pen, including fluorometry [4], spectrophotometry [5], chemiluminescence [6], electrochemistry [7] and chromatography [8]. Among them, fluorometry based on fluorescent materials has attracted much attention due to its adequate sensitivity, convenience and simplicity [4]. Several fluorescent nanomaterials, such as quantum dots [9], have already been applied as the fluorescent nanoprobes for determination of D-pen. However, organic fluorophores usually exhibit poor bio-compatibility and photo-stability [10]. Moreover, some of the fluorescent nanoprobes have exhibited the time-consuming preparation process [11] and displayed the limited bio-application due to their large size and potential toxicity. In this regards, it is desirable to explore a green and simple fluorometry method for D-pen detection by integrating with gold nanoclusters (AuNCs).

Usually, AuNCs consist of gold cores and protecting ligands or stabilizers-based shells. Interestingly, both of the gold core and the stabilizers shells of AuNCs can be used as the recognition sites. Due to the facile preparation, high fluorescence quantum yield, superior catalytic activity and excellent photo-stability [12], AuNCs have attracted great attention. Importantly, the proteins based stabilizers could effectively reduce the possible toxicity and dramatically enhance the bio-compatibility of the AuNCs. Hence, proteins-stabilized AuNCs are widely applied in environmental, biochemical, and pharmaceutical analysis [13]. However, till now, monitoring D-pen in metabolic process using proteins-stabilized AuNCs as the fluorescent probe and integrating with “turn-on” approach [4] in metabolic process has been rarely explored.

In this work, fluorescent papain-stabilized AuNCs (papain@AuNCs) with near-infrared emission have been prepared by taking a convenient one-pot synthetic approach. In the presence of copper ions (Cu2+), the fluorescence of papain@AuNCs has been quenched. Interestingly, the fluorescence of papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex could be restored upon the addition of D-pen. Subsequently, this selective and sensitive fluorescent response has been developed as a unique fluorescence turn-on assay for real time detection the serum D-pen in rats after intraperitoneal injection.

Manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2), zinc sulfate (ZnSO4), sodium chloride (NaCl), acetic acid sodium salt (NaAC), iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium chloride (KCl), copper nitrate hydrate (Cu(NO3)2), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), l-glutathione (GSH), silver nitrate (AgNO3), mercury chloride (HgCl2), sodium sulfide (Na2S) and calcium chloride (CaCl2) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Company (Shanghai, China). Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), sodium sulfite (NaHSO3), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were bought from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). Peroxynitrite (HOONO) was prepared according to the reported method [14]. Amino acids were obtained from Shanghai Huixing Biochemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Papain was gotten from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and D-pen was bought from Beijing Innochem Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Male Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats (250 ± 10 g) were obtained from Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co, Ltd, (Beijing, China). All chemicals and solvents were of analytical grade. Water used was purified using a Milli-Q-system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

All fluorescence measurements were performed on an F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a TU-1900 UV–vis double-beam spectrometer (Purkinje General, China). The TEM imaging was observed with a transmission electron microscope (TECNAI G2 F20) (FEI, America) at a voltage of 200 kV. The sample for TEM measurement was prepared by adding the solutions onto copper wire mesh. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by an FT-IR spectrophotometer (TENSOR-27, Germany). The samples for FT-IR were prepared by batch-off mill. The size distribution and zeta potential of the AuNCs were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) with a Zetasizer laser particle analyser (Zetasizer Nano ZS ZEN3600, British).

All glassware used in the experiments were cleaned in a bath of freshly prepared aqua regia (HCl:HNO3 volume ratio = 3:1), and rinsed thoroughly in doubly distilled water prior to use. Specifically, 2.5 mL HAuCl4 solution (10 mM) was mixed with 2.5 mL papain solution (50.0 mg/mL) under vigorous stirring at room temperature. After incubated at 90 °C for 5 min, 0.8 mL NaOH (1.0 M) solution was introduced into the sample solution and further incubated for 15 min. The obtained papain@AuNCs was purified by centrifugation at 10, 000 rpm for 10 min. At last, papain@AuNCs was stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

Firstly, 1.0 mM Cu2+ solution was prepared in 40.0 mM sodium acetate buffer (pH 5.5). Then, 200.0 μL of as-prepared papain@AuNCs solution was mixed with 100.0 μL of 1.0 mM Cu2+ solution at room temperature. Subsequently, 100.0 μL of different concentrations of D-pen was added into the mixture. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded. All of the fluorescence intensity in this study was measured repeatedly for three times (n = 3).

For researching the metabolic process of D-pen in rat, the fluorescence of papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex after adding D-pen was measured in the rat serum samples at different times after intraperitoneal injection. The injecting D-pen to rats has been ethically approved and made by following the ethical guidelines of the institute. Three male SD rats (about 250 g) were selected for D-pen metabolism study. In detail, the control serum samples and five different serum samples at 15 min, 30 min, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h were collected after 400.0 mg/kg D-pen sulfate dissolved in physiological saline solution (0.9%) was injected into the abdominal cavity of rats. In 1.5 mL microcentrifuge tubes, 120.0 μL of the rat serum samples were mixed with 180.0 μL of 40.0 mM sodium acetate buffer solution (pH 5.5). The solution was boiled in a water bath for 30 min and then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min. Further, 100.0 μL of the supernatant was transferred to a fresh tube containing 200.0 μL of papain@AuNCs solution and 100.0 μL of 1.0 mM Cu2+ for fluorescent spectra measurement.

It's well known that proteins are the biocompatible stabilizers for preparation of AuNCs [13,14]. Papain, an endolytic cysteine protease from carica papaya latex, has a single polypeptide chain with 212 amino acid residues, including cysteine, asparagine and histidine residues [15]. The protease should be a good candidate of protein based stabilizers for synthesis of florescent AuNCs. In this study, papain@AuNCs were thus prepared successfully by stirred the mixture of HAuCl4, papain and NaOH solutions at 90 °C for 15 min and the schematic illustration of the synthetic process is depicted in Fig. S1 (Supporting information). As described in the reference [16], the sulfhydryl group of cysteine residues could help formation of the papain@AuNCs through the Au-S bond. Other amino acid residues were more likely to be involved in the initiation and controlled growth of the papain@AuNCs though polar-polar interactions and hydrogen bonding interactions, which might be weaker than the Au-S bonding [17].

The conditions for synthesis of the intensely red-emitting papain@AuNCs were optimized, including the concentrations of papain, HAuCl4 and NaOH, synthetic temperature and time (Fig. S2-Fig. S6, Supporting information).

As described in Fig. S2 (Supporting information), the fluorescence intensity of papain@AuNCs gradually increased with the increase of papain concentrations from 25.0 to 50.0 mg/mL. While, the papain concentration ranged from 50.0 to 75.0 mg/mL caused no additional increase in the fluorescence intensity. Therefore, when the molar ratio of HAuCl4 to papain was kept at 1:1, papain concentration of 50.0 mg/mL was selected for further investigation. Then, the concentrations of NaOH were studied from 0.10 M to 0.25 M in the synthesis of papain@AuNCs. As displayed in Fig. S3 (Supporting information), the fluorescence intensity of papain@AuNCs reached a maximum value when 0.14 M of NaOH was used.

Then, the synthetic temperature for obtaining the papain@AuNCs was investigated at ranging from 80 °C to 100 °C. As depicted in Fig. S4 (Supporting information), we found that the fluorescence intensity of papain@AuNCs increased with the temperature increasing from 80 °C to 90 °C and then decreased greatly from 95 °C to 100 °C. The strongest fluorescence intensity of papain@AuNCs could be obtained at 90 °C which related to the thermal stability of papain. Therefore, the synthetic temperature at 90 °C was selected finally for further study.

Next, the synthetic time for getting papain@AuNCs was studied from 5 min to 60 min. As depicted in Fig. S5 (Supporting information), it could be observed that the fluorescence intensity of the papain@AuNCs gradually increased with the prolonged synthetic time, and reached a maximum value at 15 min. The result indicated that comparing with the reported work [11], the synthetic time in this study is much shortened.

In addition, the effect of the buffer pH (5.0–9.0) on the fluorescence intensity of the papain@AuNCs was investigated. Fig. S6 (Supporting information) indicates that the highest fluorescence intensity of the papain@AuNCs could be reached at pH 5.5, and then decreased from 5.5 to 8.0. It should be mentioned that the fluorescence intensity of the papain@AuNCs slightly increased at pH 9.0, which might be caused by the isoelectric point of papain (pI = 8.75). Finally, on the basis of the properties of the potential analytes in bio-systems, pH at 5.5 was chosen for further study.

The typical emission and excitation spectra at 639 nm and 490 nm of the as-prepared papain@AuNCs were shown in Fig. 1 A. While, the characteristic surface plasmons absorption of gold nanoparticles could not be observed in the UV/Vis spectra of papain@AuNCs as shown in Fig. 1B. It depicted an apparent broadening and shift peak, indicating that the peptide chains of papain were intertwined on the surface of the AuNCs [18] and demonstrated that the papain@AuNCs was synthesized successfully.

Fig. S7 (Supporting information) demonstrates the FT-IR of the resultant papain@AuNCs. The peak at 3400 cm−1 could be ascribed to the characteristic absorption of O-H stretching vibration. While the C-O stretching vibration peaks were located at 1688 cm−1, revealing the existence of carboxylic groups [16]. The disappearance of amide band at 1650 cm−1 was observed, which suggests the possible changes in the secondary structure of papain when conjugated with the AuNCs [19–21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex for sensing D-pen
ce:section.ce:para: AuNCs usually consist of gold cores and stabilizers shells [22–24], which can be used as the binding sites. The thiolate groups of the shells are the most universal binding function sites for forming AuNCs. In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence of papain@AuNCs was quenched because Cu2+ could specifically bind to the amino acid residues of papain, which further led to the electron density decrease on the surface of the protein@AuNCs [25]. Moreover, based on Job's plot analysis [26], the data as displayed in Fig. S8 (Supporting information) indicated that papain@AuNCs reacted with Cu2+ in ratio 1:5. Interestingly, by taking the advantage of reversible competitive coordination of metal ions between the functional groups of stabilizers of AuNCs and chelating agents, the metal ions-triggered fluorescence turn-off could be restored.

The results displayed in Fig. 2 indicated that the common metal ions, glucose, lactic acid, ascorbic acid, uric acid and amino acids produced little effect on the fluorescence restoring of the papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex. It should be noted that although the structure of D-pen is very similar to Cys, Cys had little affect on the fluorescence restoring because the formed Cu2+-D-pen chelates might be more stable than Cu2+-Cys chelates. Obviously, the addition of D-pen to the papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ solution would cause a great change in the value of I – I0, indicating a good selectivity of the papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex (Fig. S11, Supporting information). Subsequently, a competition assay based on the metal ions chelating and fluorescence “turn-on” strategy was developed for high selective sensing D-pen with the constructed papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the turn-on fluorescence for sensing D-pen
ce:section.ce:para: As exhibited in Fig. 3 , the mechanism of fluorescence quenching after adding Cu2+ to the papain@AuNCs solution and the fluorescence of papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex turning-on for sensing D-pen was explored. It has been reported that Cu2+ has high formation constants with amino acids [25]. The electro-rich functional groups (e.g., -NH2, COO−) attached on the surface of protein@AuNCs would increase the fluorescence. In the presence of Cu2+, the electron-rich groups (-NH2, COO−) of the proteins would chelate with Cu2+ on the surface of the protein@AuNCs [25]. As a result, the fluorescence of AuNCs decreased due to the decrease of electron density on the surface of the protein@AuNCs [25,27,28]. Therefore, it is assumed that the fluorescence of papain@AuNCs was easily turned-off by Cu2+, which should be ascribed to the specific binding of Cu2+ on to the surface of papain@AuNCs through the high coordination ability to amino acids residues of papain. Moreover, the papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex system displayed a marvelous fluorescence enhancement in the presence of D-pen because D-pen could capture Cu2+ from the surface of papain@AuNCs and form more preferential Cu2+-D-pen chelates (Fig. 3).

The DLS data (Fig. 4 A) showed an average size of papain@AuNCs was about 5.7 ± 1.7 nm. The TEM images (Fig. 4D) exhibited that the resultant papain@AuNCs formed spherical shapes and scattered well. However, after adding Cu2+ (Fig. 4E), the size of papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex (Fig. 4B) was enlarged to 6.3 ± 1.6 nm because Cu2+ could bind with the amino acid residues of the papain (Fig. 3). After addition of D-pen into the AuNCs solution, the size of the papain@AuNCs-Cu2+-D-pen complex decreased to 5.6 ± 1.6 nm, as exhibited in Fig. 4C and F. This was because Cu2+ could more easily coordinate with D-pen and formed stronger Cu2+-D-pen chelates [10] than that with papain. In other words, the fluorescence of papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ could be retorted in the presence of D-pen due to the strong interaction between Cu2+ and D-pen.

In addition, the mechanism of the effect of charge transfer on the surface of as-prepared papain@AuNCs after adding Cu2+ and D-pen was supported by zeta potential investigation. The results displayed that the zeta potential of papain@AuNCs was about −25.9 ± 4.4 mV, which revealing papain@AuNCs carried negative charges in the solution. After adding Cu2+, the zeta potential of papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ increased to −19.6 ± 3.8 mV, demonstrating the reduction of charge transferred on the surface of papain@AuNCs [29]. And then, it was found that the zeta potential of papain@AuNCs-Cu2+-D-pen decreased to −23.5 ± 5.4 mV, suggesting the increase of charge transferred on the surface of the AuNCs. The results provided a strong evidence to further explain the quench and restore fluorescence mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of D-pen in rat serum
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the quantitative determination of D-pen was evaluated by monitoring the change in the fluorescence of the papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ with the addition of various concentrations of D-pen. It should be noted that different volume ratio of papain@AuNCs and Cu2+ would effect the concentration of Cu2+ in the solution and the fluorescence restoring after adding D-pen. It was investigated and the results were exhibited in Fig. S12 (Supporting information). The data indicates that when the volume ratio of papain@AuNCs and Cu2+ was at 2:1, the fluorescence of papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex could be well recovered after adding D-pen. Therefore, the volume ratio at 2:1 was finally chosen for quantitative analysis of D-pen. As shown in Fig. 5 A, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ increased with concentration of D-pen increasing from 30.0 μM to 2.0 mM when the ratio of papain@AuNCs and Cu2+ was 2:1 (v/v). Fig. 5B revealed the linear relationship between the concentrations of D-pen (30.0, 50.0, 100.0, 300.0, 500.0, 1000.0, 2000.0 μM) and the relative fluorescence intensity (I-I0) of the papain@AuNCs-Cu2+. The linear regression equation could be expressed as I – I0 = 0.0138 [D-pen] + 7.805 with correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.991. The limit of detection was 5.0 μM (S/N = 3).

In order to evaluate the potential application of papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex system in detecting D-pen, the proposed sensing assay was utilized in the metabolic process analysis of D-pen in rat serum examples. As shown in Table 1 , the result suggested that papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex system is endowed with good recoveries. Moreover, comparing with other reported fluorescent probes, the proposed one well performed with shorter synthesis time and wider linear range for sensing D-pen (Table S1, Supporting information).

Finally, the metabolic assay of D-pen in rat serum has been investigated. As depicted in Fig. 6 , it has been observed that the concentrations of D-pen nearly reached the highest value at 15 min in the metabolic process. With the metabolic time prolonging, the concentrations of D-pen decreased gradually. After 6 h of metabolism in rat, the concentration of D-pen could be hardly detected, indicating the metabolism of D-pen was essentially completed. It is because D-pen could be transformed to its metabolites in the metabolic process and the results are consistent with the previously reported data [30].

Based on the papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex, a unique strategy of “turn on” fluorescence for rapid and selective detection of D-pen has been constructed. Obviously, the preparation of papain@AuNCs is rapid, simple and facile. Due to the surface electron transition decrease-induced fluorescence quenching principle, the fluorescence of papain@AuNCs has been selectively quenched for by Cu2+ over other metal ions. Importantly, the fluorescence of papain@AuNCs-Cu2+ complex could be greatly enhanced by adding D-pen because of forming preferential D-pen-Cu2+ complexes and subsequently releasing the quencher (Cu2+) from the surface of the papain@AuNCs. Furthermore, the practicality of the developed assay in monitoring the D-pen in rat serum in metabolic process has been demonstrated. It shows a great potential in establishment of novel fluorescence turn-on assay and in high selective detection of drugs in real biological samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 21575144, 91732103, 21727809, 21635008 and 21621062) and Chinese Academy of Sciences (QYZDJ-SSW-SLH034).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.04.014.
